findings.phase: Test 1 — write_count does not overwrite existing checkpoint
findings.note: Script exited 1 and left the existing checkpoint-file intact when called with 'write_count' and checkpoint already present. Confirmed by live run: Exit=1, checkpoint still exists=YES.

findings.phase: Test 2 — step_change clears stale artifacts before rebuilding
findings.note: Script printed 'replacing stale checkpoint (step_change)' to stderr, then deleted evidence-verdict.json, evidence-remediation.md, and evidence-paths.json before attempting rebuild. All three cleared=YES. Script then exited 1 only because must-do-summary.md does not exist in this project — the clearing logic ran correctly before that early-exit guard. This is the expected and correct behaviour.

findings.phase: Test 3 — post-write-check.sh outer guard and write_count guard
findings.note: Line 230: outer block opens with 'if [ -f "$EC_SUMMARY_FILE" ]' only — no [ ! -f "$EC_CHECKPOINT_FILE" ] guard, so step-change detection runs even when a checkpoint is already present. Line 278: write_count branch uses 'elif [ ! -f "$EC_CHECKPOINT_FILE" ] && [ "$EC_WRITES" -ge "$EC_THRESHOLD" ]' — correctly gated so repeated write_count triggers are suppressed while a checkpoint is pending. Both conditions match the stated fix.

summary: All three functional tests passed. The step_change path in create-evidence-checkpoint.sh correctly clears stale verdict/remediation/paths files before rebuilding. The write_count path correctly exits early (exit 1) when a checkpoint already exists. The post-write-check.sh hook correctly allows step-change detection to fire regardless of checkpoint state, while guarding the write_count trigger with the checkpoint-not-exists check. No regressions found.
